# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains experimental evidence about how single-agent and multi-agent LM systems perform across four agentic benchmarks, including quantitative results on architecture-task fit, coordination overhead, error amplification, predictive modeling, and stated limitations.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: method
citation: Kim et al., 2025, Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The study evaluates 180 controlled agent-system configurations across four benchmarks, five architectures, and three LLM families while standardizing tools, prompt structures, and token budgets.

## Source Excerpt

"We evaluate this across four diverse benchmarks... Using five canonical agent architectures... instantiated across three LLM families, we perform a controlled evaluation spanning 180 configurations, standardizing tools, prompt structures, and token budgets to isolate architectural effects from implementation confounds."

## Note

This is a study-design statement rather than a performance result.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: metric
citation: Kim et al., 2025, Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Their predictive coordination model achieved cross-validated R² = 0.513 on held-out data.

## Source Excerpt

"We derive a predictive model using empirical coordination metrics... that achieves cross-validated R2 = 0.513."

## Note

The source presents this as predictive performance for the fitted model.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: metric
citation: Kim et al., 2025, Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Under fixed computational budgets, tool-heavy tasks were reported to suffer disproportionately from multi-agent coordination overhead.

## Source Excerpt

"We identify three dominant effects: (1) a tool-coordination trade-off: under fixed computational budgets, tool-heavy tasks suffer disproportionately from multi-agent overhead."

## Note

The source explicitly frames this as a mechanism linking tool-heavy tasks to coordination cost.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: metric
citation: Kim et al., 2025, Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Coordination showed diminishing or negative returns once single-agent baselines exceeded an empirical threshold of about 45%.

## Source Excerpt

"We observe that coordination yields diminishing or negative returns (β = −0.408, p < 0.001) once single-agent baselines exceed an empirical threshold of ∼45%."

## Note

This is a conditional threshold claim tied to the reported experiments; it introduces a limit on when adding agents helps.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: metric
citation: Kim et al., 2025, Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Independent multi-agent systems amplified errors 17.2×, while centralized coordination limited this to 4.4×.

## Source Excerpt

"Independent agents amplify errors 17.2× through unchecked propagation, while centralized coordination contains this to 4.4×."

## Note

The source directly attributes the larger amplification in independent systems to unchecked propagation and presents centralized coordination as a containment mechanism.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: result
citation: Kim et al., 2025, Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
On parallelizable financial reasoning tasks, centralized coordination improved performance by 80.9%.

## Source Excerpt

"Centralized coordination improves performance by 80.9% on parallelizable tasks like financial reasoning."

## Note

The source reports this as a benchmark-specific relative improvement.
```

### file: evidence-07.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-07
kind: result
citation: Kim et al., 2025, Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
On dynamic web navigation, decentralized coordination outperformed centralized coordination in the reported relative gains, at +9.2% versus +0.2%.

## Source Excerpt

"Decentralized coordination excels on dynamic web navigation (+9.2% vs. +0.2%)."

## Note

The source compares benchmark-specific relative gains across architectures.
```

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-08
kind: result
citation: Kim et al., 2025, Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
For sequential reasoning tasks, every tested multi-agent variant degraded performance by 39% to 70%.

## Source Excerpt

"Yet for sequential reasoning tasks, every multi-agent variant we tested degraded performance by 39–70%."

## Note

This is a universal negative result within the tested multi-agent variants and should remain attached as a task-specific caveat.
```

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-09
kind: claim-support
citation: Kim et al., 2025, Introduction
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The source states that multi-agent systems evaluated on static benchmarks can show monotonic gains with team size, but this behavior changes on tasks requiring sustained environmental interaction.

## Source Excerpt

"Multi-agent systems that show monotonic improvement with team size on static benchmarks (reaching 89% on HumanEval with five agents) exhibit fundamentally different scaling behavior when evaluated on tasks requiring sustained environmental interaction, where coordination overhead and error propagation dynamics dominate."

## Note

This evidence attaches a contrast between static-benchmark results and agentic-task behavior; the cited 89% figure refers to prior static-benchmark evaluation context discussed by the source.
```

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: metric
citation: Kim et al., 2025, §4.3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The final predictive model correctly selected the optimal architecture for 87% of held-out task configurations.

## Source Excerpt

"The model correctly predicts optimal architectures for 87% of held-out task configurations."

## Note

The source presents this as held-out prediction accuracy for architecture selection.
```

### file: evidence-11.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-11
kind: metric
citation: Kim et al., 2025, §4.4
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Total reasoning turns scaled superlinearly with agent count as T = 2.72 × (n + 0.5)^1.724 with R² = 0.974.

## Source Excerpt

"Total reasoning turns... exhibit power-law growth with agent count: T = 2.72 × (n + 0.5)^1.724, R2 = 0.974... This super-linear exponent (1.724 > 1) reflects quadratic message complexity... creating a distinct agentic scaling regime."

## Note

The source links the superlinear turn growth to communication complexity and treats it as a resource constraint under fixed budgets.
```

### file: evidence-12.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-12
kind: incident
citation: Kim et al., 2025, Limitations
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The authors state that their benchmark set may not cover the full spectrum of agentic tasks and that larger or different environments could change confidence in the reported thresholds and scaling principles.

## Source Excerpt

"Our analysis spans four agentic benchmarks... [which] may not capture the full spectrum of agentic task characteristics... additional environments, particularly those with intermediate characteristics or novel task structures... would strengthen confidence in the identified thresholds and scaling principles."

## Note

This is a stated limitation on scope and external generalization that should remain attached to downstream use of the findings.
---

# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains experimental evidence about how single-agent and multi-agent LM systems perform across four agentic benchmarks, including quantitative results on architecture-task fit, coordination overhead, error amplification, predictive modeling, and stated limitations.

## Evidence Files

The study evaluates 180 controlled agent-system configurations across four benchmarks, five architectures, and three LLM families while standardizing tools, prompt structures, and token budgets.

## Source Excerpt

"We evaluate this across four diverse benchmarks... Using five canonical agent architectures... instantiated across three LLM families, we perform a controlled evaluation spanning 180 configurations, standardizing tools, prompt structures, and token budgets to isolate architectural effects from implementation confounds."

## Note

This is a study-design statement rather than a performance result.
```

## Source Excerpt

"We identify three dominant effects: (1) a tool-coordination trade-off: under fixed computational budgets, tool-heavy tasks suffer disproportionately from multi-agent overhead."

## Note

The source explicitly frames this as a mechanism linking tool-heavy tasks to coordination cost.

Coordination showed diminishing or negative returns once single-agent baselines exceeded an empirical threshold of about 45%.

## Source Excerpt

"We observe that coordination yields diminishing or negative returns (β = −0.408, p < 0.001) once single-agent baselines exceed an empirical threshold of ∼45%."

## Note

This is a conditional threshold claim tied to the reported experiments; it introduces a limit on when adding agents helps.
```

The source directly attributes the larger amplification in independent systems to unchecked propagation and presents centralized coordination as a containment mechanism.

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---

source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The source states that multi-agent systems evaluated on static benchmarks can show monotonic gains with team size, but this behavior changes on tasks requiring sustained environmental interaction.

## Source Excerpt

"Multi-agent systems that show monotonic improvement with team size on static benchmarks (reaching 89% on HumanEval with five agents) exhibit fundamentally different scaling behavior when evaluated on tasks requiring sustained environmental interaction, where coordination overhead and error propagation dynamics dominate."

## Note

This evidence attaches a contrast between static-benchmark results and agentic-task behavior; the cited 89% figure refers to prior static-benchmark evaluation context discussed by the source.
```

### file: evidence-11.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-11
kind: metric
citation: Kim et al., 2025, §4.4
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Total reasoning turns scaled superlinearly with agent count as T = 2.72 × (n + 0.5)^1.724 with R² = 0.974.

## Source Excerpt

"Total reasoning turns... exhibit power-law growth with agent count: T = 2.72 × (n + 0.5)^1.724, R2 = 0.974... This super-linear exponent (1.724 > 1) reflects quadratic message complexity... creating a distinct agentic scaling regime."

## Note

The source links the superlinear turn growth to communication complexity and treats it as a resource constraint under fixed budgets.
```

The authors state that their benchmark set may not cover the full spectrum of agentic tasks and that larger or different environments could change confidence in the reported thresholds and scaling principles.

## Source Excerpt

"Our analysis spans four agentic benchmarks... [which] may not capture the full spectrum of agentic task characteristics... additional environments, particularly those with intermediate characteristics or novel task structures... would strengthen confidence in the identified thresholds and scaling principles."

## Note

This is a stated limitation on scope and external generalization that should remain attached to downstream use of the findings.
```
